coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and cell imaging applications of amphiphilic AIE-active poly(amino acid)s
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Jianwen
Jiang, Ruming
Gao, Peng
Xu, Dazhuang
Mao, Liucheng
Zeng, Guangjian
Liu, Meiying
Deng, Fengjie
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: The poly(amino acid)s based biomaterials have attracted great research attention over the past few decades because of their biocompatibility, biodegradability and well designability. Although much progress has achieved in the synthesis and biomedical applications of poly(amino acid)s, the synthesis of luminescent poly(amino acid)s has been rarely reported. In this work, novel amphiphilic luminescent poly(amino acid)s with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) feature have been synthesized by a new approach of controlling N-carboxy anhydride (NCA) ring-opening polymerization, in which hydrophobic 2-(4-aminophenyl)-3-(10-hexadecyl-4H-phenothiazin-3-yl)acrylonitrile (Phe-NH2) with AIE feature was used as initiator and hydrophilic oligomeric glycol functionalized glutamate (OEG-glu) NCA was acted as monomer. The successful synthesis of final Phe-OEG-Pglu polymers was confirmed by different characterization techniques. Phe-OEG-Pglu polymers possess amphiphilic properties and can self-assemble into luminescent polymeric nanoparticles (LPNs). Based on cellular imaging experiments, we demonstrated that Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs have great potential for bio-imaging applications due to their attractive properties including strong fluorescence intensity, great water dispersibility, excellent biocompatibility and high cellular uptake efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation-induced emission
AIE-active poly(amino acid)s
Ring-opening polymerization
Fluorescent polymeric nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Confocal microscopic imaging of cells using Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016, 21474057, 21564006, 21561022, 21644014), and the National 973 Project (No. 2011CB935700).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 1H NMR spectrum of OEG-glu (D2O), Phe-OEG-Pglu (CDCl3) and Phe-NH2 (CDCl3).
TEM image of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs. The scale bar=200nm.
FT-IR spectra of OEG-glu, OEG-glu NCA, and as-prepared Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs.

(A) The absorption spectrum of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs, inset: the image of Phe-OEG-Pglu dispersed in water using “AIE” as background; (B) the fluorescent spectrum of Phe-OEG-Pglu dispersed in aqueous solution.
FL spectrum of Phe-OEG-Pglu in mixture solution of THF/H2O with different water fraction; λ=475nm.
The fluorescence spectra of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs before and after irradiating by UV light for 1h.
Cell viability of A549 cells. A549 cells incubated with 10, 20, 40, 80 and 120μgmL−1 of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs for 10 and 24h, respectively.

CLSM images of A549 cells after they were incubated with 40μgmL−1 of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs for 3h. The cells were excited with 458nm laser. (A) Fluorescent image; (B) bright field; (C) merged image of A and B. Scale bar=20μm.
Schematic representation of the preparation of Phe-OEG-Pglu polymers and their application for cell imaging.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and cell imaging applications of amphiphilic AIE-active poly(amino acid)s

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The poly(amino acid)s based biomaterials have attracted great research attention over the past few decades because of their biocompatibility, biodegradability and well designability. Although much progress has achieved in the synthesis and biomedical applications of poly(amino acid)s, the synthesis of luminescent poly(amino acid)s has been rarely reported. In this work, novel amphiphilic luminescent poly(amino acid)s with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) feature have been synthesized by a new approach of controlling N-carboxy anhydride (NCA) ring-opening polymerization, in which hydrophobic 2-(4-aminophenyl)-3-(10-hexadecyl-4H-phenothiazin-3-yl)acrylonitrile (Phe-NH2) with AIE feature was used as initiator and hydrophilic oligomeric glycol functionalized glutamate (OEG-glu) NCA was acted as monomer. The successful synthesis of final Phe-OEG-Pglu polymers was confirmed by different characterization techniques. Phe-OEG-Pglu polymers possess amphiphilic properties and can self-assemble into luminescent polymeric nanoparticles (LPNs). Based on cellular imaging experiments, we demonstrated that Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs have great potential for bio-imaging applications due to their attractive properties including strong fluorescence intensity, great water dispersibility, excellent biocompatibility and high cellular uptake efficiency.

Luminescent polymeric nanoparticles with aggregation induced emission feature were prepared via a ring-opening polymerization using amino-containing AIE dye (Ph-NH2) as initiator and hydrophilic oligomeric glycol functionalized glutamate (OEG-glu) N-carboxy anhydride as monomer.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent materials have caused tremendous research interest not only for their remarkable performance but also for their multifunctionality. These fluorescent materials could be used in numerous fields, including organic photoelectric conversion, bio/chemo-sensor and biomedicine [1–4]. Over the past few decades, many kinds of fluorescent materials such as semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), Ln ions doped nanomaterials, fluorescent proteins, fluorescent carbon dots, metallic nanoclusters and luminescent polymeric nanoparticles (LPNs) have been developed and widely investigated for biological applications [5–10]. Among them, fluorescent inorganic materials (e.g. QDs, fluorescent carbon dots) are of bright and stable fluorescence [11]. However, there are several inevitable obstacles that impeded their development for biological applications. For example, QDs are toxic to living cells due to the existence of heavy metal ions. Furthermore, these fluorescent inorganic nanoparticles are likely to accumulate in reticuloendothelial systems. Therefore, it would not only impact the imaging efficiency but also cause damage to living organisms. Some other biocompatible probes such as fluorescent proteins are expensive, hard to be synthesized and easy photo-bleaching [12–16]. Thus, LPNs have received increasing attention for biomedical applications owing to their easy preparation and manipulation, well designability, intense and stable fluorescence, biocompatibility and degradability potential. However, some other barriers such as aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) problem of conventional organic dyes such as perylene, fluorescein and rhodamine, which was caused by π-π stacking interactions or some other non-radioactive pathways, have truly beset scientists for quite a long time [17]. Although great effort has been devoted to overcoming the ACQ effect of conventional organic dyes based LPNs, the outcome has demonstrated to be depressed and fruitless. Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) is an abnormal phenomenon, which suggested that organic dyes with AIE feature will emit more efficiently in high concentrations and aggregation state [18–24]. It is exactly opposite with the ACQ effect and provided an effective route to conquer the ACQ effect of conventional organic dyes.

Since first discovery of the AIE phenomenon by Tang's group, a number of LPNs based on organic dyes with AIE feature have been fabricated recently [13,18,25–30]. These fabrication methods can be mainly divided into chemical and physical strategies. For instance, we reported a rather facile non-covalent method for encapsulating an AIE-active fluorogen (termed as An18) with commercial surfactants (F127), and formed a relative stable AIE dye based LPNs in aqueous solution. Results further showed that they are rapidly self-assembled, non-toxicity, water soluble, easy to be up taken by cells and high luminous efficiency. All of these features endow the emerging AIE dye based materials of great promise for biomedical applications. The AIE dye based LPNs covalently fabricated by employing different polymerization methods and variety of functional monomers were also developed [31–33]. For example, we reported a facile method for preparing PEGylated and cross-linked LPNs by using reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization. These prepared AIE-active LPNs have uniform sizes, excellent dispersibility, photostability, biocompatibility as well as high luminous efficiency. The results of biocompatibility evaluation and cell uptake behavior experiments suggested that they are very suitable for biological imaging applications. More recently, polyethyleneimine cross-linked AIE dye based LPNs with bright yellow emission were also reported. The preparation was first taken out through the radical polymerization between the polymerizable AIE monomer (termed as Phe) and 2-isocyanatoethyl methacrylate [34]. Then these polymers were cross-linked by the PEI to obtain the novel low toxic fluorescent polymers. The amphiphilic polymers also have the novel characteristics mentioned above. By contrast, the LPNs prepared by covalent methods are much more stable owing to their remarkable structure properties, and they should be more desirable for real applications [26,35–37].

Functionalized poly(amino acid)s have recently attracted increasing research interests in constructing various well-defined materials. The excellent biocompatibility, easy preparation and many other admirable performances such as stimuli-responsive make them promising for a variety of applications such as controlled drug/gene delivery [38–48]. The advisable functionalization of poly(amino acid)s could help to enhance their solubility and biocompatibility while most natural water soluble poly(amino acid)s are polyelectrolytes, which lead to pH-dependent solubility and unsatisfied for biological applications. Furthermore, the charged characteristic indicates they would bind with oppositely charged biomolecules and also impact their applications in vivo [49–54]. Numerous of functionalized poly(amino acid)s based materials have been developed. For example, Li et al. have developed a biomimetic one-pot methodology for preparing water dispersible non-spherical silica nanoplates [55]. Rory et al. reported a rapidly formed supramolecular poly(amino acid)s–DNA hydrogel, which was promising for 3D bioprinting [56]. However, the synthesis of AIE-active poly(amino acid)s has not been demonstrated thus far. Polypeptide is as one of environmentally stimuli-responsive polymers because their properties can respond to external stimuli such as temperature, pH, light, ions and biomolecules. Synthetic AIE-active polypeptides as promising biomaterials maybe display unprecedented properties in contrast to traditional AIE polymers.

In this work, the design, synthesis and biomedical imaging applications of AIE-active poly(amino acid)s have been reported for the first time. The OEGylated amino acid (termed as OEG-glu) and corresponding α-amino acid N-carboxy anhydride (termed as OEG-glu NCA) were prepared via literature method and subsequently an amino group-containing AIE dye was employed to initiate the ring-opening (RO) polymerization between OEG-glu NCA. The final AIE-active LPNs (named as Phe-OEG-Pglu) were fully investigated by a series of standard characteristic methods (Scheme 1 ). Further biological assays demonstrated that Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs possess good biocompatibility and cell imaging efficiency. All the results suggest the successful construction of AIE-active OEGylated poly(amino acid)s and their promising properties for biomedical applications.

All the chemicals such as methoxy polyethylene glycol350 (OEG), phenothiazine, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), 1,2-dichloroethane, phosphoryl chloride, 4-aminobenzyl cyanide were purchased from commercial sources of analytical grade and used directly without further purification unless extra mentioned.

1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded on Bruker Avance-400 spectrometer with deuterated dimethylsulfoxide as the solvent. The synthetic polymers and materials were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) using KBr pellets, the FT-IR spectroscopy was supplied from Nicolet5700 (Thermo Nicolet corporation). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a Hitachi 7650B microscope operated at 80kV. The TEM specimens were got by putting a drop of the nanoparticle ethanol suspension on a carbon-coated copper grid. The fluorescence data was obtained from the Fluorescence spectrophotometer (FSP, model: C11367-11, Hamamatsu). The absolute fluorescent quantum yields were analyzed using the fluoroSENS instrument (Gilden Photonics). The excitation wavelength was 472nm (2-nm band pass) and the emission spectrum from 500 to 700nm was recorded (2-nm band pass). Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) analyses of polymers were performed using N,N-dimethyl formamide (DMF) containing 50mM LiBr as the eluent. The GPC system was a Shimadzu LC-20AD pump system consisting of an auto injector, a MZ-Gel SDplus 10.0μm guard column (50×8.0mm, 102 Å) followed by a MZ-Gel SDplus 5.0μm bead-size column (50–106 Å, linear) and a Shimadzu RID-10A refractive index detector. The system was calibrated with narrow molecular weight distribution polystyrene standards ranging from 200 to 106 gmol−1.

The monomers OEG-glu and OEG-glu NCA were prepared as literature reported [57]. Phe-NH2 was prepared based on our previous report [58]. The Phe-OEG-Pglu polymers were prepared via the RO polymerization, in which Phe-NH2 was used as the amino-containing agent to initiate OEG-Pglu NCA. Briefly, 90mg Phe-NH2 and 300mg OEG-glu NCA were added into a 100mL flask and sealed with N2, then 25mL DMF was injected into the reaction system. After stirred for 8h under room temperature, the final product was precipitated and washed with anhydrous diethyl ether. The resultant product was dried in a vacuum oven at 40°C.

As is well known, the fluorescent nanoparticles have promising biomedical application potential such as cell imaging, drug delivery vehicle and targeting trace. In this work, the successful preparation of Phe-OEG-Pglu LNPs could be described in follows: the synthetic Phe-OEG-Pglu polymer (10mg) was dissolved in the mixed solution of THF (5mL) and distilled water (10mL) by the supersonic treatment for 10min. After then, the THF solvent was removed via the rotary evaporation for 10min, and the Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs could be obtained because of the aggregation of hydrophobic segment of AIE dye as core while hydrophilic polymer extend into water as shell. Thus-obtained Phe-OEG-Pglu LNPs solution was stored in the shade place for further TEM characterization.

The cell viability of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs on A549 cells was evaluated by cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay based on our previous reports [59,60]. Briefly, cells were seeded in 96-well microplates at a density of 5×104 cellsmL−1 in 160μL of respective media containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). After 24h of cell attachment, the cells were incubated with 10, 20, 40, 80 and 120μgmL−1 Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs for 10 and 24h. Then cells were washed with phosphate buffered solution (PBS) three times to remove noninternalized nanoparticles. 10μL of CCK-8 dye and 100μL of Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) cell culture medium were added to each well and incubated for another 2h at 37°C. Plates were then analyzed with a microplate reader (VictorШ, Perkin-Elmer). Measurements of formazan dye absorbance were carried out at 450nm, with the reference wavelength at 620nm. The values were proportional to the number of live cells. The percent reduction of CCK-8 dye was compared to controls (cells not exposure to Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs), which represented 100% CCK-8 reduction. Three replicate wells were used per microplate, and the experiment was repeated three times. Cell survival was expressed as absorbance relative to that of untreated controls. Results are presented as mean±standard deviation (SD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal microscopic imaging of cells using Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs
ce:section.ce:para: A549 cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated FBS, 2mM glutamine, 100UmL−1 penicillin, and 100μgmL−1 of streptomycin. Cell culture was maintained at 37°C in a humidified condition of 95% air and 5% CO2 in culture medium. Culture medium was changed every three days for maintaining the exponential growth of the cells. On the day prior to treatment, cells were seeded in a glass bottom dish with a density of 1×105 cells per dish. On the day of treatment, the cells were incubated with Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs at a final concentration of 40μgmL−1 for 3h at 37°C. Afterward, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove the Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10min at room temperature. Cell images were taken with a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) Zeiss 710 3-channel (Zeiss, Germany) with the excitation wavelength of 458nm.

Although great effort has devoted to the design and synthesis of AIE-active LPNs and revealed their different applications, the functionalized poly(amino acid)s based LPNs have seldom reported. In the current work, we reported the fabrication of AIE active OEGylated poly(amino acid)s with unique optical feature and admirable biocompatibility based on RO polymerization. In this reaction system, the amino group-containing AIE dye (Phe-NH2) and subsequently employed as the initiator for the RO polymerization of an OEGylated glu-NCA. To verify the successful synthesis of OEG-glu and Phe-OEG-Pglu, the 1H NMR spectra of OEG-glu and Phe-OEG-Pglu were first conducted. As shown in Fig. 1 , a series of typical signal peaks could be observed. For example, the clear signal peaks from 2.93 to 3.66ppm can be ascribed to the protons of OEG. The peaks located from 0.65 to 1.55ppm can be assigned to the protons of glutamic acid. The above results suggested that OEG has successfully conjugated with glutamic acid through formation of ester bonds. Furthermore, the signal peak at 8.27ppm is possibly corresponding to the protons of –NH2 of OEG-glu, also suggests the successful reaction between OEG and glutamic acid. From the 1H NMR spectra of Phe-OEG-Pglu, the signal peaks of OEG and glutamic acid could all be observed. These signal peaks located from 6.5 to 8.5ppm can be attributed to the protons of aromatic rings of Phe-NH2. This demonstrated the successful construction of the AIE-active Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs through RO polymerization.

To determine the molecular weight of Ph-OEG-Pglu, the ESI-MS spectrum of this product is provided. As shown in Fig. S1, when Ph-OEG-Pglu (10mg) was dissolved in the acetonitrile solvent, there are many signal peak could be detected. It can be contributed the existence of many polymers with different molecular weight by the ring-opening polymerization. The strongest signal peak majorly appeared at ranging from 1751 to 2059, we can thus conclude that the molecular weight of fabricated Ph-OEG-Pglu polymer is about 1905Da. Due to the molecular weight of Phe-NH2 is about 565Da, therefore, we can calculate the average polymerization degree of OEG-glu NCA is about 3. On the other hand, M w of Phe-OEG-Pglu was also characterized by GPC is 1706gmol−1 and the PDI of the polymer is relatively narrow (1.06). The molecular weight detected by GPC is very close to that of mass spectroscopy (Fig. S2).

The self-assembly of amphiphilic Phe-OEG-Pglu polymers in aqueous solution was first evidenced by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 , many spherical particles with size ranging from 150 to 300nm could be clearly observed. The formation of spherical nanoparticles in aqueous solution indicated that successful synthesis of Phe-OEG-Pglu polymers. Among these self-assemblies, the hydrophobic AIE dye will be encapsulated in the core, while the hydrophilic OEG-Pglu will be covered on the hydrophobic core and expanded into water. As a result, the final Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs displayed high water dispersibility and strong emission in pure aqueous solution.

To further investigate the structure of the functional monomers and final Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs. FT-IR spectra were used to confirm successful preparation of Phe-OEG-Pglu fluorescent materials (Fig. 3 ). As compared with these spectra of three samples (OEG-glu, OEG-glu NCA and Phe-OEG-Pglu), we can demonstrate that successful preparation of Phe-OEG-Pglu micelles and its intermediates. For example, we can first observe an incisive absorption peak at around 1100cm−1, which belongs to the stretching vibration of OCH2 CH2 O from OEG, suggesting successful synthesis of OEG-glu NCA. On the other hand, the strong absorption peaks ranging from 2841 to 3012cm−1 belong to the CH stretching vibration. Obvious changes can be observed from Phe-OEG-Pglu and OEG-glu NCA. This difference indicates the successful RO polymerization of Phe-NH2 with OEG-glu NCA because of the existence of alkyl chain in Phe-NH2. What's more, the absorption peak of OEG-Pglu at 1693cm−1 can be corresponded to the vibration of carbonyl group of ester, meanwhile, the peak at 1632cm−1 is ascribed to the carbonyl group of COOH. As seen the IR curve of OEG-glu NCA sample, the peak intensity of COOH at 1632cm−1 is weak, and new weak peak at 1702cm−1 is first discovered, which is contributed to the stretching vibration of carbonyl of anhydride, confirming the successful preparation of OEG-glu NCA. On the other hand, according to the IR curve of Phe-OEG-Pglu fluorescent polymer, the peak at 1632cm−1 is emergence, which demonstrated prosperous RO reaction between Phe-NH2 and OEG-glu NCA and formation of carboxyl groups, further evidencing the successful fabrication of Phe-OEG-Pglu. In addition, an absorb peak at 2214cm−1 mainly ascribe to the vibration of the CN from Ph-NH2 also proved the successful construction of Phe-OEG-Pglu.

The amphiphilic structure of Phe-OEG-Pglu polymers makes them trend to self-assemble into nanoparticles in aqueous solution, while the hydrophobic AIE dye would aggregate into cores and the hydrophilic PEG extend into water to form shells. The uniform size and PEG shells also endow them enhanced biocompatibility and make them easily be internalized by cells. To confirm these opinions, other characterized measurements and biological assays such as UV–Visible and photoluminescent (PL) spectra, cell viability and cell uptake behavior of these LPNs were carried out. As shown in Fig. 4A, the UV–Vis spectrum of amphiphilic Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs demonstrated the formation of nanoparticles in distilled water. Three peaks located at 211, 252 and 426nm was found in the UV–Vis spectrum of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs. The absorption peak at 211nm should be assigned to the π→π* transition of aromatic rings. The absorption peak at 252nm can be ascribed to the aromatic rings conjugation with NH2, which will cause the obvious red-shift of absorption peak of aromatic rings. Furthermore, the incisive absorption peak at 426nm can be assigned to the conjugated structure (π-π) of Phe-NH2. The UV–Vis spectrum provides powerful evidence for the successful synthesis of Phe-OEG-Pglu polymers via a sample RO polymerization because the OEG-glu NCA is lack of absorption peaks in these regions. On the other hand, The PL spectra of these AIE-active Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs dispersed in aqueous solution were shown in Fig. 4B. From the PL spectra, we can find that the maximum emission wavelength of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs is located at 570nm, while the maximum excitation wavelength is located about 472nm. Furthermore, the good symmetry of the emission signal peak also further proved that the AIE dye possesses higher signal-noise ratio. The inset of Fig. 4A is the photography of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs, which were dispersed in pure aqueous solution. It can be seen that no obvious aggregation was found and the background mark of the cuvette was also clearly observed. This implied that Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs can be well dispersed in aqueous solution. When Phe-OEG-Pglu water suspension was irradiated by UV lamp (λ=365nm), uniform and strong orange-red luminescence can be found. This is well consistent with the PL spectra of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs. Based on the above results; we can draw a conclusion that these Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs is promising for bioimaging applications considered their good water dispersibility and high emission efficiency. On the evaluation of the fluorescence quantum yield of Phe-OEG-Pglu, used the single-photon counting and computer-controlled fluorescence spectrometer, the absolute fluorescent quantum yield (Ф) is 20.7% with 472nm excitation wavelength.

Phe-OEG-Pglu was confirmed as AIE materials by the detection of their AIE feature. As shown in Fig. 5 , we studied their photoluminescence behavior when Phe-OEG-Pglu (1mg) was dissolved in the THF (20mL) as mother solution. The fluorescence intensity of Phe-OEG-Pglu is weak when dissolved in the THF solution, and increase slowly until water fraction arrived 60%, afterward, the emission intensity was strengthened swiftly. The emission intensity was enhanced drastically when water fraction increased to 90%, suggesting unique AIE feature of Phe-OEG-Pglu.

As is well-known, the photostability of LPNs is excellent important for their potential imaging application. Because of the great light resistance of AIE dyes, numerous attentions thus have been paid to construction of LPNs with high brightness, uniform size and desirable biocompatibility for their potential biological applications such as in vitro/vivo imaging and long-term cancer cell tracing. In this work, we developed a facile strategy to prepare amphiphilic Phe-OEG-Pglu luminescent materials via a simple RO polymerization using synthetic Phe-NH2 as initiator and OEG-glu NCA as monomer. The obtained amphiphilic Phe-OEG-Pglu would self-assemble into nanoparticles. Because of AIE dyes (Phe-NH2) aggregated as core of polymeric nanoparticles, making Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs with excellent photostability. Therefore, the photostability measurement of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs dispersed in water was carried out as shown in Fig. 6 . The fluorescence intensity of original Phe-OEG-Pglu water solution was 831a.u., after irradiating in the UV light for 1h, the fluorescence intensity of LPNs solution only reduced to 793a.u., the decreased data of FL intensity is about 38a.u., which could demonstrate that great photostability of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs. This unique photostability make these LPNs have promising application for long-term bioimaging.

In order to further evaluate the potential biological imaging application of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs, the biocompatibility of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs was evaluated via incubating A549 cells with different concentrations of LPNs for 10 and 24h. The survival quantity of A549 cells was calculated based on CCK-8 assay and results were shown in Fig. 7 . The results suggested that there is almost no changes of the cells viability values after A549 cells were incubated with various concentrations of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs for 10 and 24h. The cell viability is still greater than 97% when the concentration of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs is 120μgmL−1. The cell viability values are even greater than 100% in the concentrations is less than 80μgmL−1. This is likely ascribed to the interference of internalized Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs on cell viability determination. As evidenced by the UV–Vis spectrum of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs, a strong peak at 426nm can be found. More importantly, there still has considerable absorption at the wavelength 450nm. The above results provide powerful evidence that Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs possess excellent biocompatibility. Combination the great water dispersibility, strong fluorescence and excellent biocompatibility of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs, we expect these AIE-active LPNs could serve as ideal candidates for various biomedical applications. More importantly, the method described in this work is rather sample and high-efficiency (room temperature condition, catalysis free and no by-products), which offer an inspiration for fabrication of AIE active dyes based nanoprobes.

The cell uptake behavior of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs was also evaluated using CLSM observation. As shown in Fig. 8 , intense fluorescent signal could be observed in the cells under the microscopy after A549 cells were incubated with Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs at a concentration of only 40μgmL−1 for 3h, demonstrating these Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs prepared in this work could facilely enter into cells and are efficient for cell imaging applications. More interestingly, there are some relative rounded dark areas in the cells (Fig. 8A). This can be explained that Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs cannot enter into cell nucleus and are mainly distributed in cytoplasm. It also indicates that Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs would not cause damage to DNA of cells after they were employed for biological imaging. Fig. 8B is the bright field of cells. It can be seen that cells are still kept their normal morphology and strongly adhered onto cell plates. This also implied that Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs possess good biocompatibility. Fig. 8C is the merged image of Fig. 8A and B. We can find that the fluorescent signal is exactly covered on the locations of cells and the center of cells is absent of fluorescent signal. This suggested that the Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs are indeed internalized by cells. Furthermore, the internalization mechanism of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs by A549 cells should be attributed to the non-specific endocytosis because no targeting agents were immobilized on these LPNs. That is to say, the dosage for cell imaging applications would be further decreased if Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs were functionalized with targeting agents such as folic acid, antibodies or aptamers etc. Therefore, based on the outstanding properties of Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs, we could expect that Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs have great potential for various biomedical applications [61–82].

In summary, we have demonstrated the first example of employing amino group-containing AIE dye with strong orange-red emission to initiate OEG-glu NCA to synthesize AIE-active poly(amino acid)s via RO polymerization. The successful preparation of these amphiphilic AIE-active poly(amino acid)s was confirmed by a number of characterization techniques in detail. Because these amphiphilic Phe-OEG-Pglu polymers are constructed with hydrophobic AIE dye (Phe-NH2) and hydrophilic polymers (OEG-Pglu), they could self-assemble into AIE-active LPNs in aqueous solution. The aggregation of hydrophobic AIE dye in the core will result in their strong fluorescent emission. While hydrophilic polymers covered on Phe-OEG-Pglu LPNs will endow their good water dispersibility. More importantly, the strategy described in this work is facile, high-efficient and well designable. We are pretty sure that the new method described here would attract much interest on fabricating high performance AIE-active LPNs, which displayed high well designable chemical structure and functional groups, high water dispersibility, strong luminescence and desirable biocompatibility.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016, 21474057, 21564006, 21561022, 21644014), and the National 973 Project (No. 2011CB935700).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2017.05.090.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mat. Sci. Eng. C Mater. Biol. Appl.
